coredata.dc:title: Distinctive sensing nanotool for free and nanoencapsulated quercetin discrimination based on S,N co-doped graphene dots
coredata.dc:creator: Montes, Cristina
Villamayor, Natalia
Villaseñor, M. Jesus
Rios, Angel
coredata.dc:description: A selective and sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe (sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots, S,N-GQDs) was designed for both detection and discrimination between free and quercetin-loaded nanoemulsion in food samples of diverse nature. Quercetin nanoemulsions (Q-NEs) were synthesized by a phase inversion temperature (PIT) procedure, while S,N-GQDs were synthesized using a bottom-up methodology by means of simple hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and cysteamine. Both synthetized nanomaterials (analyte and fluorescent probe), were carefully characterized through advanced spectroscopic and high-resolution microscopic techniques. It was observed that fluorescence intensity of S,N-GQDs could be markedly and distinctively quenched by the addition of both quercetin forms through inner filter effect (IFE) mechanisms, exhibiting static quenching events for free quercetin and Q-NEs but with a characteristic 13 nm red-shift spectra in presence of Q-NEs. Linear dynamic ranges between 0.05 and 10 mg L−1 and 0.025–70 mg L−1, with detection limits of 17 and 8 μg L−1 were revealed for free and nanoquercetin, respectively. After nanostructural and physic-chemical optimization, the discrimination strategy was metrologically validated and applied to nutraceutical supplements containing nanoencapsulated quercetin and on diverse free quercetin products such as onion peels and dietary supplements. Accuracy and reliability were proved by means of a statistical comparison with the results obtained by a μHPLC-DAD method (paired Student's t-test at 95% confidence level). The method provides the exciting prospect of analyzing new designed nanoencapsulated bioactives without altering their native nanostructure, as well as being able to accomplish the challenge to distinguish between both forms of quercetin.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Q-NEs: A) DLS number distribution diagram; B) SEM image of Q-NEs; C) CM images in absence and presence (inset) of irradiation; D) UV–Vis spectra of Q-NEs with Tween 80, Q-NEs with Tween 20, empty nanoemulsion (NE-blank) and free Q; E) (left) Raman spectra of individual components and (right) the same for empty NE (red dashed line) and Q-NEs (grey solid lines). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Characterization of S,N-GQDs by A) UV–Vis and fluorescence (dashed and dotted line: excitation and emission spectra; solid line: absorption spectra); B) TEM image and lattice spacing (inset); C) XRD pattern and D) FTIR spectra.

A) Fluorescent decay of S,N-GQDs (20 mg L−1) in absence (grey square) and presence of free (orange triangle) and encapsulated Q (red rhombus) (10 mg L−1). B) overlapping between the fluorescence excitation (dashed line) and emission (dotted line) spectra of S,N-GQDs and the absorption spectra of free Q and C) the same for Q-NEs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Interaction quenching mechanism between S,N-GQDs and A) free Q; B) Q-NEs. Fluorescence spectra and calibration plots corresponding to quenching with free Q C),E); and with Q-NEs: D),F).
Effects of potential interfering substances on the fluorescent S,N-GQDs assisted determination of a) 5 mg L−1 of free quercetin and b) 35 mg L−1 of quercetin nanoemulsions.
Optimization of components and their percentages for the quercetin nanoemulsion synthesis. All results were performed in triplicate.
Comparison of different analytical methods for quercetin detection with the proposed fluorescent S,N-GQDs assisted one.

Analytical results of both free and Q-NEs in nutraceutical supplements and onion peels by fluorescence and HPLC techniques. Statistical comparison between results from both methodologies (α = 0.05, n = 5). *t crit(α = 0.05, 4° of freedom, 2 tails) = 2.78

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A selective and sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe (sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots, S,N-GQDs) was designed for both detection and discrimination between free and quercetin-loaded nanoemulsion in food samples of diverse nature. Quercetin nanoemulsions (Q-NEs) were synthesized by a phase inversion temperature (PIT) procedure, while S,N-GQDs were synthesized using a bottom-up methodology by means of simple hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and cysteamine. Both synthetized nanomaterials (analyte and fluorescent probe), were carefully characterized through advanced spectroscopic and high-resolution microscopic techniques. It was observed that fluorescence intensity of S,N-GQDs could be markedly and distinctively quenched by the addition of both quercetin forms through inner filter effect (IFE) mechanisms, exhibiting static quenching events for free quercetin and Q-NEs but with a characteristic 13 nm red-shift spectra in presence of Q-NEs. Linear dynamic ranges between 0.05 and 10 mg L−1 and 0.025–70 mg L−1, with detection limits of 17 and 8 μg L−1 were revealed for free and nanoquercetin, respectively. After nanostructural and physic-chemical optimization, the discrimination strategy was metrologically validated and applied to nutraceutical supplements containing nanoencapsulated quercetin and on diverse free quercetin products such as onion peels and dietary supplements. Accuracy and reliability were proved by means of a statistical comparison with the results obtained by a μHPLC-DAD method (paired Student's t-test at 95% confidence level). The method provides the exciting prospect of analyzing new designed nanoencapsulated bioactives without altering their native nanostructure, as well as being able to accomplish the challenge to distinguish between both forms of quercetin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: sulfur and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: The growing interest which food has aroused together with certain social and economic conditions have generated a series of transformations within the Science of Nutrition, giving rise to the develop of new products and concepts. This is the case of nutraceutical foods, covering a wide variety of products, including flavonoids [1–3]. Flavonoids belong to the broad family of polyphenols, found in many foods, and already known for their antioxidant ability. Quercetin (Q) is in turn a natural flavonoid becoming the object of emerging lines of research due to its undeniable advantages in the field of health, for instance, its beneficial effects related to its anti-cancer, immunological and cardiovascular activity [4,5]. Each Q molecule contains five hydroxyl groups whose presence determines the biological activity of this compound [6]. It is found in a great number of food products and plants, including fruits, seeds, and vegetables [7]. However, its incorporation into foods as a nutraceutical is limited due to its hydrophobicity, hence the interest on its encapsulation, with the aim of increasing its solubility and bioavailability [8]. There have been reported so far numerous studies in which Q has been encapsulated in different formulations of lipid nanocarriers to improve both properties, for example nanoemulsions [9–13], nanoliposomes [14,15], lipid nanoparticles [16–18], etc. Unfortunately, the lack of rigorous analytical and toxicological controls on these nanometric formulations prevent to know potential adverse effects due to their small size and rapid diffusion throughout the body, specifically above a certain dose. Therefore, to guarantee consumer safety, it is of great importance to develop exhaustive stability studies to expect evolution of these nanoformulations in food samples as well as their corresponding analytical and nanotoxicological evaluations [1].

So far there has been reported the use of different techniques for determination of free Q, such as voltammetry [19], mass spectrometry [20], colorimetry [21], spectrophotometry [22], capillary electrophoresis [23], liquid chromatography [24], gas chromatography [25] and Raman spectroscopy [26], but however, most of these methods require specific qualified staff and long analysis times. In addition, it is important to stand out that all mentioned researches just focus on evaluation of unencapsulated Q. All these reasons justify the need to develop rapid and sensitive analytical methods providing differentiation and quantification of free and nanoencapsulated Q attending to informative and straightforward techniques such as fluorescence ones. These techniques are especially suited due to their sensibility as well as the chance of performing indirect and non-destructive determinations by means of diverse fluorescent nanometric sensors. As it is already described, Q has an extremely weak native fluorescent emission intensity to be used in direct quantitative analysis especially at low levels, which is caused by the depletion of the excited energy associated to its intramolecular rotational vibration along the bond between B and C rings (Fig. 1 D). That is the reason why those works found in the bibliography reported indirect fluorimetric determinations of free Q [27], but instead, no references were discovered for nanoencapsulated Q. This fact opened us two research possibilities, on the one hand, the first one based on the need to develop analytical methodologies that allowed discerning and quantifying between both forms of this bioactive in a reliably way. On the other hand, a second one, whose leading aim lied on providing analytical strategies that avoided altering the original structure of nanoformulations as occurs with other methodologies such as chromatographic or electrophoretic ones. To implement both purposes, it was designed and synthesized a fluorophore such an S,N-doped graphene quantum dots whose quenching interactions allowed to achieve it.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have been widely used in fluorescence, replacing conventional fluorophores because of their lower toxicity, easier synthesis, and greater luminescence. Between them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained great interest within scientific community due to its high aqueous solubility, tunable photoluminescence (PL) and biocompatibility [28]. GQDs are graphene sheets sized between 2 and 10 nm, which exhibit unique optical and electronic properties because of their quantum confinement and edge effects [29]. GQDs preparation methods can be classified into bottom-up and top-down methods. Top-down methods show several limitations such as low throughput, critical synthesis conditions and difficulty to control the size distribution. In contrast, bottom-up methods allow to control the morphology and size distribution with greater precision. Their procedures are usually based on the carbonization of specific organic precursors, such as citric acid, by heat treatment [29]. Despite their excellent properties, raw GQDs with unmodified surface exhibit a low fluorescence quantum yield, which is a limitation for their use as fluorescent sensors with analytical purposes. To overcome this disadvantage, the GQDs surface is usually modified with different functionalities containing diverse heteroatoms such as B, N, S, F and P, seeking to improve their optical and electronic properties and, consequently, the fluorescent quantum yield as well. This enhancement in the quantum yield is related to the increase in defective/reactive states and additional energy levels when introducing heteroatoms at specific sites [30]. For the determination of free Q, GQDs single-doped with S (S-GQDs) [28], with N (N-CDs) [31], and with B (B-GQDs) [27], have been successfully tried until date. Between their advantages as doping agents it should be highlighted their atomic sizes similar to carbon atoms, more specially for N, and also their fitting electronegativities enabling charge transfer processes [29,31], thus make them especially well suited as potential substituents. Specifically, with regard to the presence of dopant S atoms on GQDs surface, the satisfactory results can be explained attending to HSAB theory (“Hard and Soft Acids and Bases theory”), since the phenolic hydroxyl groups present in chemical structure of quercetin belong to soft acids category whereas the relatively high polarizable S donor atom belong to soft bases category, leading to the formation of a complex between the fluorophore and the quencher in the ground state [28]. By other hand, the advantages of N as a dopant atom derive from the fact of increasing the quenching chances, since it exhibits a suitable electronegativity to give rise to processes by charge transfer [32], as already mentioned. However, the simultaneous combination of S and N functionalities as doping agents, despite its excellent perspectives to improve the fluorescent quantum yield, has not been evaluated yet for the quantification of free Q, whereas the determination of nanoencapsulated quercetin has not been addressed at all until date by any fluorescence approach. Thus, our research has tried to enhance the synergistic effect between both doping atoms with the aim of increasing both sensitivity and selectivity (very important) to discriminate between free quercetin and Q-NEs (quercetin-loaded nanoemulsion).

In this work, a fluorescent nanometric sensor has been applied for the first time for both discrimination and quantification of free and nanoencapsulated Q, without destroying the native nanostructure of this last one as great challenge. Specifically, GQDs were synthesized and modified with S and N functionalities for this purpose. With the same aim, Q was entrapped in nanoemulsions (Q-NEs), designed with components Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS) within the food field, and attending to a hereby optimized PIT (Phase Inversion Temperature) procedure [33]. Lastly, this work also intends to contribute to the development of the novel third way of analytical nanometrology using nanomaterials both as tools and as the main object of analysis.

All the used reagents were “analytical grade” quality. Miglyol 812 oil, quercetin, polyethylene glycol sorbitol monolaurate (Tween 20), polyethylene glycol sorbitan monooleate (Tween 80), 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES hydrate), sodium hydroxide, ethanol, cysteamine hydrochloride, citric acid, ammonium chloride, ammonium hydroxide, L-Ascorbic acid, hydrochloric acid, acetonitrile, glacial acetic acid, magnesium stearate, microcrystalline cellulose, titanium oxide, sodium chloride, copper chloride, magnesium sulfate, cholecalciferol, tocopherol, and calcium chloride were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich, whereas soy lecithin (Ractapur Prolabo) was acquired from Dilabo.
All solutions and analytical grade reagents above listed were prepared using 18.2 MΩ cm (25 °C) ultrapure water obtained with a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

An ultrasound bath (Selecta, Barcelona, Spain), a BioSan high-speed mini-centrifuge Microspin 12 (LabNet Biotecnica S.L., Spain) and a pH-meter (Crison Instruments S.A., Spain) were used at different experimental stages. Absorption spectra of Q-NEs, quercetin and S,N-GQDs solutions were achieved with a SECOMAM UVI Light XS 2 spectrophotometer equipped with a LabPower V3 50 using 10-mm quartz cuvettes.

Particle size distribution, polydispersity index and zeta potential of Q-NEs were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements using a Zetasizer Nano Series DLS ZEN3500 (Malvern Instruments Limited, Spain). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of Q-NEs were taken with a ZEISS GeminiSEM 500 microscope to evaluate their shape, size, and morphology. Real-time imaging of Q-NEs in solution was performed with a ZEISS fluorescence confocal microscope (Vert.A1-Axiocam 702 mono) in absence and presence of light. This microscope is equipped with a fluorescence laser with emission filter centered at 530 nm and two objective lenses from 10x to 40x for magnification. The solution containing the nanosized emulsion was inserted into a microfluidic channel and recorded with and without irradiation. The structural characterization of Q-NEs was also conducted by Raman spectroscopy on a portable BWTek i-Raman (Model # BWS465–785S), equipped with a standard BCR100A accessory for liquid phase measurements in a common cuvette. This equipment includes a laser wavelength of 785 nm and maximum output laser power of 348 mW at the excitation port.

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) micrographs of S,N-GQDs were performed on a Jeol JEM 2011 operating at 200 kV and equipped with an Orius Digital Camera (2 x 2 Mpi). The digital analysis of the HRTEM micrographs was achieved using Digital Micrograph TM 1.80.70 for GMS 1.8.0 Gatan. The samples were prepared onto a lacey carbon/format-coated copper grid by drop depositions of the synthesized material suspension. XRD (Difraction X Ray) patterns to characterize GQDs were measured on Philips model X'Pert MPD diffractometer using a CuKα source (l = 1.5418A°), with programmable divergence slit, graphite mono-chromator and proportional sealed xenon gas detector. The sample was measured with an angular range from 5° to 50° (2θ), a step of 0.013° 2θ and a time per step of 1 s. Infrared spectra of S,N-GQDs (solid powder) were recorded with a crystal ATR (Attenuated Total Reflectance) of ZnSe Shimadzu, IRAffinity-1S model, and DTGS Standard detector in the range between 500 and 4000 cm−1. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a QuantaMaster40 spectrofluorometer from Photon Technology International equipped with a 75 W continuous xenon short-arc lamp using a detection voltage of 981 V. The SOC-10 USB interface and the FelixGX software were used to collect and process fluorescence data. The spectra were recorded between 200 and 600 nm to check the optical fluorescence properties of S,N-GQDs. Both emission and excitation slit widths were 0.1 nm with integration of 0.3 s unless otherwise specified. All optical experiments were performed at room temperature using quartz flow-through cuvette with layer thickness 2 x 10 mm2.

An Agilent Technologies HPLC (High Performance Liquid Chromatography) system (series 1200, Waldbronn, Germany) was utilized for the chromatographic analysis, which were performed on a Zorbax SB-C18 column (5 μm particle size ODS, 150 × 0.5 mm) from Agilent. This system consists of a solvent reservoir, a degasser, a pump, an autosampler, a thermostatted column compartment and a 1260 Infinity model diode-array detector.

Q-NEs were synthesized upon a previously described PIT [13] method, here adapted with some modifications. The first stage consisted of mixing all the components of the nanosystem in a beaker, with magnetic stirring at 360 rpm: 10% w/w Miglyol 812, 0.25% w/w quercetin, 0.55% w/w EtOH (ethanol), 7.4% w/w Tween 80, 0.1% w/w soy lecithin and 81.7% w/w aqueous phase (MES, 2-ethanesulfonic acid, pH = 5.9), being the percentages above indicated those finally considered as optimal ones. Subsequently, this early mixture was treated through three cycles of temperature inversion, as follows: a) initial temperature rise on the heating plate from 10 °C to 80 °C, where it was kept stirring for 20 min to favor its stabilization and b) later rapid cooling until 10 °C by immersing the beaker in an ice bath with maintained stirring at 360 rpm for another 20 min at said temperature. Finally, the obtained solution was centrifuged for 40 min at 5000 rpm to separate free Q (precipitated at the bottom of the eppendorf) and quercetin-loaded nanoemulsions (supernatant) which was extracted with a syringe.

S,N-GQDs were synthesized by a single step method, based on the procedure described by Qu et al. [34] with some modifications, through a bottom-up approach by a facile hydrothermal process with citric acid and cysteamine hydrochloride. Initially, a 5 ml aqueous solution containing 0.23 g of cysteamine, and 0.23 g of citric acid was prepared and stirred until to obtain a clear solution. Later, this clear solution was sealed in a Teflon lined autoclave reactor and heated in an oven at 160 °C for 4 h. Then, the reactor was taken out and cooled to room temperature. Finally, the obtained solid powder was reconstituted in 5 ml of ethanol (yellow solution). In this step, a large part of the impurities precipitated while S,N-GQDs remained in the supernatant. Subsequently, S,N-GQDs solution (250 g L−1) was filtered using a 0.45 μm nylon filter and diluted with ultrapure water to obtain a work concentration of 1 g L−1.

Commercial dietary supplements containing nanoencapsulated quercetin were acquired from the international market (One Planet Nutrition and ActiNovo). The bioactive commercial product with declared free quercetin (Solaray) was obtained through local distributors. All these products belong to recognized brands and their declared composition was:
Nano Quercetin, capsules (One Planet Nutrition, EE.UU.): Aqueous Nano Quercetin, vegetable capsule, rice Flour. It does not contain soy, corn, yeast, wheat, gluten, milk, egg, fish, shellfish.
Liposomal Quercetin, liquid (Actinovo, Germany): Purified water, phospholipids, acid, and natural sea buckthorn extract.
Quercetin 500 mg (Solaray), vegetable cellulose capsule; anti-caking agent: stearic acid and silica dioxide; bulking agent: cellulose.

The developed fluorescence methodology was applied to above-described quercetin samples: two dietary supplements with claimed nanoencapsulated quercetin, a third supplement with declared free quercetin and a fourth sample, a fresh vegetable which was a simple red onion.

For those commercial products nanoquercetin-containing, namely Actinovo Nanoquercetin, a 1: 100 dilution was prepared in MilliQ water while for One Planet Nutrition, the content corresponding to one capsule (0.5 g) was dissolved in 250 mL of MilliQ water and stirred for 15 min. After that, for both samples, an aliquot (2 mL) was taken and centrifuged for 30 min at 5000 rpm to remove unencapsulated quercetin and potential aggregates. Then the supernatant was transferred to an Eppendorf; at this stage, the size of the encapsulated nanoquercetin droplets was determined by DLS, while the quercetin content within nanoemulsions was quantified using the developed fluorescence methodology. For this, 70 and 165 μL of Actinovo and One Planet Nutrition samples respectively were mixed with 4 μL of the S, N-GQDs working nanoprobe solution and the corresponding volume of 0.1 M MES pH 5 until to complete a final volume of 200 μL.

For the commercial sample free quercetin-containing, Solaray, one capsule (0.5489 g) was dissolved in 250 mL of EtOH. The few anti-caking granules were allowed to decant, then an aliquot was taken, and 440 times diluted in EtOH. Then, 100 μL of this diluted solution, 4 μL of the S,N-GQDs working solution and the remaining volume up to 200 μL of 0.1 M MES were taken and submitted to fluorescence measurements. Regarding the onion sample treatment, firstly quercetin was extracted by mixing 0.9286 g with 2 mL of castor oil and let it stirring for 3 h at 50 °C. After that, 1 mL of this oil was taken to prepare a 5 mL ethanolic solution. Next, the quercetin content was determined by mixing 5 μL of this last ethanolic solution, 4 μL of S,N-GQDs probe solution and the remaining with 0.1 M MES up to 200 μL, later applying the proposed fluorescence methodology.

Fluorescence measurements were performed at room temperature using 1-cm quartz cuvettes. Test solutions were obtained by mixing 4 μL of S,N-GQDs (1 g L−1), the corresponding volume to obtain the desired concentration of Q or Q-NEs and the remainder until completing 200 μL with 0.1 M MES adjusted at pH 5. Fluorescent spectra were recorded from 360 to 600 nm using as suitable parameters: excitation wavelength of 345 nm, excitation and emission slit widths of 0.1 nm, step size of 2 nm and integration of 0.3 s. The maximum of fluorescent emission intensity for free Q was located at 415 nm while the fluorescence intensity for Q-NEs was collected at those wavelengths corresponding to their maximal intensities.
The optimal composition was selected attending to the results obtained regarding the nanodroplets size, polydispersity index (PdI) and entrapment efficiency (EE) along all the performed experiments.

Miglyol 812 was selected as lipid phase. This oil is a medium chain triglyceride, whose influence related to their weight percentage within nanoemulsions was studied between 5.25 and 15%. The results in terms of hydrodynamic size, PdI and EE showed a slight decrease in the mean diameter of the nanodroplets and a significant increase in EE as its mass percentage increased (Table 1 ). Finally, 10% was the percentage of Miglyol 812 selected as the most suitable as a compromise solution between acceptable values of both PdI and EE. Regarding the surfactant, several types were used, namely Tween 20, Tween 80, and soy lecithin. On the one hand, a non-ionic synthetic surfactant, Tween 20, was selected for its safety and usefulness to produce oil-in-water emulsions in the food industry upon bibliography [10]. It is also reported that high concentrations of surfactant imply the formation of nanoemulsions with smaller droplet size. However, the obtained results when the percentage of Tween 20 increases did not show such behavior (Table 1). This may be due to the generation of other types of nanostructural aggregates (potentially nanohydrogels) between oil, water, Tween 20, and bioactive molecules; thus, Q remained retained and free within the originated network but without being encapsulated as a nanoemulsion. In fact, this hypothesis was confirmed by analyzing the resulting UV–Vis spectra corresponding to the supernatant fractions. Therefore, it was necessary to look for another non-ionic synthetic surfactant, Tween 80, whose selected mass percentage value was 7.5% according to the obtained EE, PdI and droplet size results (Table 1).

Attending to the growing demand in the food industry to manufacture delivery systems based on natural surfactants, a natural one such as soy lecithin was also incorporated. This surfactant is considered as GRAS type by FDA (Food & Drugs Administration); however, it must be used in combination with other surfactants [35], due to its exhibited low short-term stability to form nanoemulsions. Different weight percentages of soy lecithin in combination with Tween 20 and Tween 80 were evaluated between 0.5 and 2% and 0.1–0.5% respectively, being 0.1% the optimal percentage mainly attending to PdI results. It was observed the higher the soy lecithin percentage was, the greater the coalescence and the polydispersity index, and consequently, the smaller the nanoemulsion stability.

Moreover, ethanol was employed as suitable co-solvent to improve structural properties like interfacial tension and solubility. Its w/w percentage, together with the optimal one for Q bioactive, were evaluated in a previous research works performed in the group [33].

Regarding the stability of the Q-NEs and with the aim to achieve low flocculation and polydispersity events, the selected pH in the aqueous phase was 5.9. This pH was supplied with MES (2-ethanesulfonic acid), which is widely used as biological buffer [36].

Table 1 summarizes the results for all experiments carried out along the development of this optimization synthesis process, also displaying the final selected composition: 10% w/w Miglyol 812, 0.25% w/quercetin, 0.55% w/w ethanol, 7.4% w/w Tween 80, 0.1% w/w soy lecithin and 81.7% w/w of aqueous phase (pH = 5.9, MES 10 mM).

By DLS, it was possible to evaluate parameters such as hydrodynamic size, polydispersity index and zeta potential. The obtained results finally considered as optimal ones for the Q-NEs showed a size of 10.41 ± 1.09 nm (n = 3) (Fig. 1A), as well as 0.25 ± 0.01 (n = 3) and −3.88 ± 0.11 (n = 3) in terms of PdI and zeta potential, respectively. Also, the pseudo-spherical geometry of Q-NEs was confirmed by SEM, as it can be seen in Fig. 1B. Finally, the Q-NEs solution 50 times diluted was introduced into a microchannel and excited with a 530 nm laser to irradiate the sample for visualizing its state in aqueous medium. So, Fig. 1C demonstrated how the nano-droplets were well dispersed in the aqueous media in the absence and presence (inset) of irradiation.

The successful bioactive entrapment as well as a preliminary discrimination between free and encapsulated Q were initially assessed with the help of UV–Vis spectroscopy. The UV–Vis (Ultraviolet–Visible) spectra corresponding to the Q-NEs solutions, the precipitate (free Q) and the blank (nanoemulsion without Q) are displayed in Fig. 1D. On one hand, the spectrum of free Q shows two bands with maxima at 256 nm and 376 nm corresponding to the absorption of rings A and B respectively [37]. The NE blank (empty nanoemulsion) present a single absorption maximum at 229 nm. On the other hand, two peaks with maximal absorption appear in the Q-NEs spectrum, at 229 and 376 nm respectively. It must be highlighted such 229 nm peak since its presence allows to discern between Q-NEs (with 229 and 376 nm maximal absorption bands) and free or unencapsulated Q (with maximal absorption at 256 and 376 nm). Thus, this detailed study of spectra appearance allowed us to conclude that interaction between Q and the rest of the nanoemulsion components took place through A ring.

Regarding the spectrum obtained for those Q-NEs prepared with Tween20, two maxima can be appreciated at 376 and 256 nm which agree with those presented by free Q. Furthermore, the maximum located at 229 nm, responsible for the interaction of ring A with the other components of the nanoemulsion, does not appear in this spectrum. Both facts prove its unsuccessful encapsulation in such conditions as already advanced in the previous section. Finally, UV–Vis spectrophotometry was also employed to perform a linear regression fit of Q in ethanol to get EE percentages, as described in section S1 of Supplementary Material (SM) file. This linear regression fit was: y = (7.21·10−2 ± 2.36·10−3)·x (mg·L−1) + (1.35·10−1 ± 1.54·10−2), r2 = 0.995 (Fig. S1) and excellent EE values (99.8%) were reached for Q-NEs in the optimal synthesis conditions.

Raman spectroscopy was also applied to carry out the structural characterization of the Q-NEs. Firstly, all nanoemulsion components were individually characterized, as shown in Table S1 (SM file). Raman spectrum of Q-NEs is displayed in Fig. 1E (right), where four typical Q bands at 600, 1569, 1616 and 1656 cm−1 are shown, corresponding to the stretching modes of C=O, C2=C3, the rest of C=C, and to the skeleton out-of-plane bending of C–C from the A and C rings [38]. It is also important to highlight that these peaks do not appear in the blank spectrum of nanoemulsion (red line). Stretchings corresponding to the rest of nanoemulsion components can be observed in both spectra (nanoemulsion blank and Q-NEs spectra) in the range from 720 to 1500 cm−1. As conclusion of this detailed study, food grade Q-NEs were successfully synthesized as they showed good stability over a period of at least 30 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanostructural and morphological characterization of S,N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As it is already known, the hydrothermal process can partially carbonize citric acid to prepare GQDs which present small group of isolated sp2 within the graphitic structure [28,30]. To improve the optical and electronic properties of surface state and the local chemical structure, cysteamine was used to selectively doping GQDs with sulfur and nitrogen atoms using a bottom-up approach. To assess the successful S,N-GQDs synthesis, their optical properties were firstly evaluated. The so synthesized S,N-GQDs exhibited high aqueous solubility and emitted blue light under UV lamp at 365 nm wavelength. Their UV–Vis absorption spectrum showed two bands. A small absorption band located at 240 nm, due to the π -π* electronic transitions of C=C bonds in the sp2 aromatic carbons of synthesized nanoparticles and a strong absorption band at 343 nm corresponding to n- π* electronic transitions of the oxygenated functionalities (C=O, C–OH) existing on sheets edges [39]. The displayed UV–Vis spectra exhibits a slight shift (±10–15 nm) for these absorption bands regarding other consulted bibliographic works, which is likely due to its doping with heteroatoms, reason why additional energy states are formed, thus occurring changes in their electron transition pathways and consequently in their associated absorption bands [29]. Moreover, it was possible to conclude that the absorption band located at 345 nm corresponded to optimal fluorescent excitation peak of S,N-GQDs [40]. Thus, for such excitation conditions the maximal emission intensity was obtained at 415 nm (Fig. 2 A) with a quantum yield of 71% [34].

The size distribution and morphological characterization of S,N-GQDs was investigated using HRTEM. Fig. 2B shows a TEM image where the S,N-GQDs distribution display nanoparticles with spherical shape, uniform size, and good dispersity. Size distribution swings between 3 nm and 6 nm being the average size 4.8 ± 0.43 nm (n = 79). The HRTEM image of S,N-GQDs shows that many of the synthesized S,N-GQDs exhibit a high degree of crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.28 nm (inset Fig. 2B) which denote the lattice fringes (1120) of graphene.

The structural characterization was carried out by XRD and FTIR (Fourier Transformed Infra-Red). Fig. 2C shows a XRD pattern with a broad peak around 24° which is due to the (0 0 2) plane of graphite that agrees with highly disordered graphene-like structures, in accordance with other works previously reported for this type of nanomaterial [40]. A FTIR study was surveyed to know the featured chemical composition of S,N-GQDs. Fig. 2D displays broad absorption bands between 2700 and 3100 cm−1 corresponding to the O–H and N–H bonds; therefore, the presence of amino, hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups on the surface of S,N-GQDs can be confirmed, and they are responsible of their hydrophilic character. A small band typical of the S–H bonds appears at 2596 cm−1. Two characteristic bands for stretching vibrations of carbonyl from carboxylic groups and of N–H bindings are noticed at 1697 and 1625 cm−1, respectively. An intense typical band of the C=C bending can be also observed at 1500 cm−1. Moreover, the bands corresponding to the stretching vibrations for C–O–C, C–N and C–S also appear at 1242, 1143 and 645 cm−1, respectively. Thus, the FTIR survey allowed to confirm the presence of doping nitrogen and sulfur functionalities [34,41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of physic-chemical conditions for fluorescent detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence intensity can be affected by several conditions such as pH of media, buffer nature and concentration, incubation time, kinetic behavior, and S,N-GQDs concentrations; for this reason, the influence of these variables was evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH study: nature and concentration of buffer
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, it was important to consider the physicochemical stability of the analytes and nanoprobes under consideration in a wide pH range. For this, a study with buffers from different nature, such as: HCl, MES, NH3/NH4OH and NaOH (0.1 M all of them), was carried out. The stability of both quercetins (free and encapsulated one) at different pHs, was evaluated by UV–Vis spectroscopy since it does not exhibit fluorescence (Fig. S2A in SM file). The results showed that free Q (10 ppm) was stable at acidic and neutral pH being the pH 2 which displayed highest absorption. However, changes in the morphology of the UV–Vis spectra were observed from pH 8 which denoted structural alterations of the analyte. Similarly, Q-NEs were studied and revealed a similar behavior than the free form (Fig. S2B in SM). Likewise, the fluorescent intensity of a 25 mg L−1 S,N-GQDs solution was evaluated in the same pH range (Fig. S2C). Their emission spectra displayed greater intensity at weakly acidic pHs, specifically between 4 and 5, decreasing for higher pH values. This increase in the emitted fluorescent intensity is due to the protonation processes of carboxylate and sulfinyl groups existing on the GQDs surface [28]. Finally, a pH 5 value was selected as a compromise choice for the study of both analytes and sensing nanoprobes.

The interaction between analytes and the S,N-GQDs surface can be also influenced by the ionic strength of media (buffer concentration). So, the next step addressed the influence of MES concentration (pH 5), between 0.01 M and 0.5 M values, on S,N-GQDs solutions in presence of free and encapsulated quercetin. The linear fits and regression coefficients obtained along this experience are summarized in Table S2. From them and attending to sensitivity criteria, values of 0.15 M and 0.1 M MES showed the best results for both free Q and Q-NEs determination; moreover, it must be noted that higher sensitivity was always achieved for the nanoencapsulated analyte. Finally, a 0.1 M MES concentration was selected with the perspective in mind of a discrimination quenching based.

Accurate evaluation of this parameter must be afforded since changes in the fluorescent intensity of the nanoprobe may be originated by short lifetimes and not by variations in analytes concentrations. So, evaluation of time stability of S,N-GQDs was accomplished to achieve reproducible and proper results along the development of fluorescent method. With this aim, the incubation time of this sensing system was monitored along a 20 min period by contact with free and loaded quercetin (before excitation step). As it can be seen in Fig. S3, the selected F0/F response increased slightly up to 5 min when interacting with Q-NEs, from this time it decreased until the highest intensity was obtained after 15 min. For unencapsulated quercetin, the lowest signal was obtained after 2 min of reaction and subsequently, the intensity increased until reaching its highest value at 5 min. Therefore, considering both analytes’ results, 5 min was selected as ideal incubation time to measure the analytes-S,N-GQDs solutions. It is important to emphasize that, although 15 min shows greater intensity than the selected time for the Q-NEs, the relationship between the global analysis time and the increased intensity is not strong enough to be considered.

A kinetic study was carried out to investigate how the reaction between free or loaded quercetin with S,N-GQDs evolved over time. For that purpose, the reaction was monitored for 20 min in presence and absence of analytes. The results reported that quenching of quercetin in both forms over S,N-GQDs quickly occurs. However, no recovery trend was observed for the nanomaterial since after quenching the intensity remained constant during the 20 min, therefore, the proposed fluorescent sensing signals are stable for almost 20 min (Fig. S2D).

Finally, the concentration of nanomaterial used as fluorescent nanoprobe was investigated since our aim was to use the lowest concentration without losing sensitivity. Then, different solutions with concentrations ranged between 5 and 25 mg L−1 were submitted to the quenching process by contact with different concentration of both analytes. In sensitivity terms, better results were again obtained for Q-NEs versus those for free Q in all checked S,N-GQDs concentrations. Otherwise, lower nanoprobe concentrations (5 and 10 mg L−1) drove to slightly worse results for the Q-NEs determination and drastically worse ones for the free analyte (Table S3). Thus, 20 mg L−1 of S,N-GQDs solution were selected as a the most suitable choice for both quercetin forms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential quenching mechanism for both free and encapsulated nanoquercetin
ce:section.ce:para: It was reported that doping carboxyl, amino, and thiol groups of fluorophore species can interact with the quencher through several physic-chemical processes such as molecular rearrangements, energy transfer, formation of ground state complexes, molecular interactions, excited-state reactions, and collisional events causing so diverse fluorescence quenching phenomena [43]. More specifically, quenching processes can be usually due to three mechanisms, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). There are three main strategies which help to discern what mechanism is responsible of a specific quenching event. If there is a change in the lifetime of the fluorophore with respect to the fluorophore-quencher complex, the originated quenching mechanism is generally PET; if there is overlapping between the fluorophore emission band and the quencher absorption band, the occurred mechanism is FRET whereas if there is a partial overlapping between both emission and excitation bands of the fluorophore with the quencher absorption band, the mechanism will be IFE type. With this aim, lifetimes of S,N-GQDs were measured in the presence and absence of free and encapsulated nanoquercetin (Fig. 3 A), and the fluorescent emission and excitation spectra of S,N-GQDs were also compared to the absorption spectra of free Q and Q-NEs to check potential overlappings (Fig. 3B and C).

On the one hand, the results indicated that PET mechanism was not here involved since changes were not observed regarding to the lifetimes of fluorophore (9.27 ± 6.5·10−2 ns) and those of the two complex fluorophore-quencher species (9.22 ± 7.1·10−2 ns and 9.11 ± 5.9·10−2 ns for free and Q-NEs, respectively). On the other hand, although the superposition of spectra does not show strong evidences, it can be clearly ruled out a quenching caused by FRET mechanism since the absorption spectra for both quenchers is much closer to the excitation spectrum (Δλ = 31 nm) than to the emission spectrum (Δλ = 39 nm) of fluorophore. The fact the quenchers absorptions bands are very closer to that one of the excitation fluorophore but do not completely overlap can be due to the displacement of S,N-GQDs band because of their surface modifications as already explained in section 3.3. From the above comments, the S,N-GQDs fluorescent quenchings induced by both free and encapsulated Q are associated to an IFE mechanism. This assessment is also consistent with findings from other researches where free Q was studied by means of different fluorescent probes [7,28,30,31,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination between free and encapsulated quercetin
ce:section.ce:para: Once elucidated the chemical/spectroscopic nature of involved quenching phenomena, it was tried to discriminate between free Q and Q-NEs, through their distinctive displayed quenching behaviors. Since there are no changes in the fluorescence lifetimes of the fluorophore and of the quencher-fluorophore complexes as commented in the previous section, the quenching mechanism associated to both processes is a static quenching (Fig. 3A). So, both analytes interact with the nanoprobe through an inner fitter effect mechanism (IFE), forming non fluorescent ground-state complexes (later detailed), which immediately return to the ground state without photonic emission when the complex absorbs light (reason why there are no changes in the fluorescence lifetimes) [30,43,45], according consulted bibliography. Besides, the fluorescent response of the S,N-GQDs probe was evaluated for successive increasing concentrations of each Q form. The displayed results showed a distinctive red-shift of 13 nm for S,N-GQDs in presence of nanoquercetin (Fig. 4 D). This means that graphene layers containing the diverse functionalities of the fluorophore, interacted distinctively with both analyte environments driving to a red-shift emission spectra in Q-NES presence, which suggest that the Q-NEs quenching mechanism is not just related to an inner filter effect process, but also may be due to the fundamental state of the complex formed [7,46]. Thus, the final emission spectra result from the contribution of both factors, reason of their displayed red-shifting with the Q-NEs increasing.

To make clear the nature of interactions related to the two established ground state complexes, the zeta potential values of S,N-GQDs solutions were measured in presence and absence of free and loaded nanoquercetin. From the evaluation of these results, it was confirmed the existence of electrostatic interactions since zeta potential of S,N-GQDs changed from −4.48 mV to −6.26 mV and −5.81 mV in presence of Q and Q-NEs, respectively [31]. Thereupon, the ground-state complexes established between Q, Q-NEs and S,N-GQDs happen through electrostatic interactions. Specifically, the amino groups (weak base) and thiol groups (donor electrons as Lewis base) present on the surface of the GQDs layers can interact with the phenolic hydroxyl groups (weak acids) of Q and with those existing in the Tween 80 polar heads of Q-NEs (Fig. 4B) through hydrogen bonds [30]. Moreover, the transition complex Q-GQDs will be further reinforced by the establishment of π stacking interactions only possible between the aromatic rings of free Q and those of S,N-GQDs graphenic layers (Fig. 4A).

The analytical performance characteristics of the fluorescent quenching strategy were evaluated by means of the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. 2), which predicts a linear dependence of the analytical signal Fo/F and the concentration of analyte under study, in this case Q-NEs or free Q. Fo and F are the fluorescence intensities of S,N-GQDs solution in absence and presence of the analyte, [Q] is the concentration of quencher (Q-NEs or free Q) and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.

According to the previously selected physic-chemical conditions, that means incubation time 5 min, 0.1 M MES (pH 5) as buffer solution and 20 mg L−1 S,N-GQDs solution as fluorescent nanoprobe, repeatability (n = 10) was firstly checked obtaining RSD (Standard Deviations Relative) values of 0.8% for both free Q and Q-NEs (10 mg L−1), while for intermediate precision (n = 8), RSD values of 5.3% and 4.2% were found for free Q and Q-NEs on two consecutive days. Dynamic linear behaviors were found in the ranges 0.025–70 mg L−1 and 0.05–10 mg L−1 for the fluorescent sensing of Q-NES and free Q respectively (Fig. 4E and F), achieving the following linear fits:

The reached LOD (Limit of Detection) values were 8 ppb and 17 ppb while the LOQ (Limit of Quantification) values were 25 ppb and 50 ppb for Q-NEs and free Q respectively. These parameters were initially calculated according IUPAC criterion [47] and experimentally verified next.

With the aim to evaluate the selectivity of the analytical discrimination strategy, interference studies were carried out on the selected analytical signal of both analytes to investigate the effects of different compounds usually present in nutraceutical/dietary supplements as well as in fresh vegetables. Then, different vitamins such as C, E, D, B2 and B6 and diverse electrolytes such as Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Cl−, SO4 2− were checked on a 20 mg L−1 S,N-GQDs solution containing also 35 mg L−1 of Q-NEs or either 5 mg L−1 of free Q in different ratios: 0:1, 0.5:1, 1:1, 2:1. The results of these experiments evidenced its suitability about the selectivity tests and they are shown in Fig. 5 . From a general point of view, appreciable variations were not observed from any interferent substance on the Q analytical signal whereas in the study performed on Q-NEs signal it was noticed a great influence of Cu2+, which produced an increase of quenching effects on S,N-GQDs solution, likely due to its already know plasmonic properties. It should be also commented the influence of lipophilic vitamins (B2, E and D) on both Q forms, being more pronounced for Q-NEs where the Fo/F signal decreased a 75% when their ratio was 1:1 whereas it was surprisingly doubled for the 2:1 ratio. Therefore, it would be necessary to develop a previous separation method for that quercetin or nanoquercetin commercial samples containing such interferences. For instance, a membrane filtration procedure in the presence of a chelating agent such as 1-2-Pyridylazo 2-naphthol (PAN) could be used to avoid Cu2+ interference [48]. On the other hand, a heat treatment could be applied to remove fat-soluble vitamins interferences as they are known to be thermally unstable [49,50], whereas such procedure does not affect quercetin. Besides other potentially interfering compounds present in these supplements, such as some stabilizers and anticaking agents, that are magnesium stearate, microcrystalline cellulose, and titanium oxide, are not soluble in aqueous medium, so they were simply removed by filtration of the samples.

Lastly, Table 2 display a comparison about the sensitivity of the presented method regarding other recently reported for Q determination evidencing the good results here achieved for both analytes. Specifically, attention should be paid to reported LODs for examples corresponding to Refs. [7,51–53], where quercetin determination was fluorometrically accomplished with raw (non-doped) GQDs. They showed significantly higher LODs with regard those obtained with doped GQDs (e.g., references [27, B-doped], [31, N-doped] and [43, N-doped] and our own research, dual doped), likely because of the absence of defective states and additional energy levels that heteroatoms bring on, which would involve a tailored band gap and strong PL properties due to quantum confinement and edge effects [30], in turn associated with quantum yield and sensitivity increases.

Furthermore, recent trends are focused into dual doping of GQDs, for instance N,S-GQDs. S,- doping plays an important role in enhancing the emission intensity of N,S-GQDs, which can be tuned by adjusting the N/S atomic ratio. In addition, their luminescence was exceptionally resistant to high salt concentration [54], upon reported literature. Attending to these advantages, we have tried to apply the synergistic effect between both doping atoms with the aim of increasing both sensitivity and selectivity (very important) to discriminate between free quercetin and Q-NEs. So, to our knowledge, although there are some studies [31,43] showing greater sensitivity, this co-doped carbon nanoparticle has provided for the first time the pioneering chance to perform non-destructive analysis of new designed nanoencapsulated bioactives without altering their native nanostructure, as well as its application in native commercial samples containing it.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to nutraceutical supplements and food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Further and with the aim to prove the accuracy and reliability of the proposed method, these samples were also analyzed by a RP-μHPLC-DAD (Reversed Phase micro–High Performance Liquid Chromatography-Diode Array Detector) method (section S3 in SM). Then, an analytical performance study was carried out by means of this methodology, resulting the linear regression fit y = (333.16 ± 5.58) x – (53.763 ± 1.94·101) with a regression coefficient of 0.9986, within a dynamic linear range from 0.1 to 7 mg L−1 (Fig. S4 of SM). For the analysis of onion peels, the Q content was achieved through the standard addition method, obtaining similar values in both cases. All the results obtained via fluorescence and RP-μHPLC-DAD analysis (n = 5) for the studied samples were reflected in Table 3, where a good agreement can be found between the contents obtained by both techniques, thus evidencing the accuracy of the raised fluorescence approach [55]. Subsequently, the paired Student test-t was performed between fluorescence and chromatographic data, following the indications of the “Statistics and chemometrics for analytical chemistry manual” by Miller [56], and obtaining in all cases t0 experimental values lower than the theoretical one 2.78 (n = 5) (Table 3). Therefore, it can be guaranteed the absence of significant statistical differences between the results obtained by both methodologies at a confidence level of 95% which would prove its reliability at that checked confidence level.

This work shows the applicability of a fluorescent quenching strategy using S, N co-doped GQDs as distinctive sensing nanotool to both discriminate and quantify free quercetin and quercetin-loaded nanoemulsions in fresh food vegetable samples and diverse commercial native nutraceutical supplements. It must be highlighted the pioneering chance to perform non-destructive analysis for achieving the exhaustive analytical characterization of Q-NEs without altering its native structure as new design nanostructured organic material. Food grade quercetin nanoemulsions were successfully synthesized with a droplet size less than 100 nm (10.41 ± 1.09) and encapsulation efficiency of 99.8 ± 0.1%. Likewise, the S, N-GQDs synthesized by a bottom-up methodology has proved to be a novel, miniaturized and effective fluorescent probe (total sensing and fluorescent probe volumes are 200 and 4 μL respectively), as well as eco-friendly and sensitive regarding the discrimination and detection of Q-NEs. The elucidation of interaction mechanism between S,N-GQDs and both forms of quercetin is associated with an inner filter effect. Thus, static quenching events based on the establishment of ground-state complexes through electrostatic (Q-NEs) and also π-π (free Q) interactions were observed, while a 13 nm red-shift was noticed for S,N-GQDs spectra in presence of Q-NEs, thus allowing its discrimination. Moreover, it is important to note that linearity range increased considerably with quercetin encapsulation because of its distinctive sensitivity related to their different interactions towards the nanoprobe in the posed analytical strategy. It must be highlighted this research is a clear example of the third way of Analytical Nanoscience and Nanotechnology which implies the use of analytical nanotools for the determination of nanosized analytes.
